Course Outline & Learning Outcomes

Bioethics: An Open Educational Resource

Author

Brendan Shea, PhD

Published

April 21, 2026

1 Course Structure

This course consists of 12 lectures, each delivered in two parts (designed for a twice-weekly schedule). Online students watch both parts as a single unit. Alongside the lectures, students read and discuss 12 case study documents drawn from historical and contemporary bioethics.

The course is organized around the four principles of biomedical ethics — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice. Major ethical theories (utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, care ethics, principlism) are introduced as lenses that ground and sometimes conflict over these principles.

2 Course-Level Learning Outcomes

By the end of the course, students will be able to:

  1. Identify and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to clinical and research scenarios.
  2. Analyze moral arguments — identifying premises, conclusions, hidden assumptions, and common fallacies.
  3. Compare how major ethical frameworks (utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, care, principlist) approach the same dilemma and yield different conclusions.
  4. Evaluate real and contemporary bioethics cases using appropriate legal, historical, and empirical context.
  5. Articulate their own reasoned position on a contested bioethical issue, engaging fairly with opposing views.

3 Lecture Topics at a Glance

# Lecture Part A Part B
1 Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning Four principles; moral arguments Theories as lenses on the principles
2 History & the Clinical Relationship Hippocratic → Nuremberg → Belmont Autonomy, consent, truth-telling, confidentiality
3 Research Ethics RCT primer; historical abuses Placebos, vulnerable populations, Global South
4 Abortion Moral status arguments Post-Dobbs landscape; feminist perspectives
5 Reproductive Technology IVF, surrogacy, commodification Savior siblings; posthumous reproduction
6 Genetics & Enhancement Genetics primer; testing CRISPR; therapy vs. enhancement
7 End of Life Brain death, advance directives Euthanasia, MAID, disability critiques
8 Justice & Health Care Allocation Theories of justice; right to care Disparities, implicit bias, rationing
9 Public Health Ethics Vaccination, liberty vs. welfare Pandemic triage; COVID lessons
10 AI in Medicine Diagnostic AI; algorithmic bias AI in mental health; liability, privacy
11 Gender, Identity & Disability Gender-affirming care; Cass Review Disability ethics; body integrity
12 Animal Ethics Moral status; animal research Factory farming; xenotransplantation

4 Lecture-Level Learning Outcomes

4.1 L1. Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning

  • Define the four principles of biomedical ethics and give a clinical example of each.
  • Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, or personal-preference claims.
  • Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose.
  • Recognize common fallacies in bioethical reasoning (ad hominem, appeal to nature, slippery slope, naturalistic fallacy).
  • Summarize the core commitments of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics.
  • Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently.

4.2 L2. History & the Clinical Relationship

  • Trace key events in the development of modern bioethics (Hippocratic oath, Nazi experiments, Nuremberg Code, Tuskegee, Belmont Report).
  • Compare the four Emanuel & Emanuel models of the clinician-patient relationship (paternalistic, informative, interpretive, deliberative).
  • Explain the four elements of valid informed consent: disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, and decision-making capacity.
  • Analyze conflicts between patient autonomy and clinician beneficence.
  • Identify legal and ethical limits of confidentiality, including Tarasoff duty-to-warn and mandatory reporting.
  • Discuss how cultural context complicates truth-telling norms.

4.3 L3. Research Ethics

  • Explain the basic structure of a randomized controlled trial (randomization, blinding, placebo, control group, intention-to-treat).
  • Distinguish research from clinical care and describe the “therapeutic misconception.”
  • Summarize lessons from Tuskegee, Willowbrook, and Nazi medical experiments.
  • Apply the Belmont principles (respect for persons, beneficence, justice) to IRB review.
  • Evaluate the ethics of placebo-controlled trials when effective treatment exists.
  • Identify vulnerable populations and the heightened protections they require.
  • Analyze ethical tensions in conducting trials in low-resource settings (e.g., short-course AZT trials).

4.4 L4. Abortion

  • Describe the post-Dobbs legal landscape and variation across US states.
  • Explain Thomson’s violinist argument and Marquis’s future-like-ours argument.
  • Evaluate criteria for moral status (biological humanity, personhood, sentience, viability).
  • Analyze reproductive justice and feminist perspectives on bodily autonomy.
  • Apply the four principles to specific cases (rape, fetal anomaly, maternal life, gestational age).
  • Assess how real-world clinical implementation of exceptions succeeds or fails.

4.5 L5. Reproductive Technology

  • Describe IVF, ICSI, PGD/PGT, and gestational surrogacy at a functional level.
  • Evaluate arguments for and against commercial surrogacy and egg donation.
  • Analyze the commodification critique (Anderson) and the procreative liberty response (Robertson).
  • Discuss “savior siblings,” posthumous reproduction, and synthetic gametes.
  • Apply the four principles to embryo selection cases (sex selection, tissue matching, disability).

4.6 L6. Genetics & Enhancement

  • Explain core genetics concepts: gene, genome, allele, germline vs. somatic cells, inheritance patterns.
  • Describe CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in accessible terms.
  • Distinguish gene therapy from genetic enhancement and assess whether the line is morally meaningful.
  • Analyze the He Jiankui (2018) case and the global response to germline editing.
  • Explain the non-identity problem and the “child’s right to an open future” argument.
  • Evaluate arguments for and against enhancement (Savulescu, Sandel, Glannon).

4.7 L7. End of Life

  • Distinguish brain death, coma, persistent vegetative state, and minimally conscious state.
  • Explain advance directives, living wills, durable power of attorney, and POLST/MOLST forms.
  • Compare active euthanasia, passive euthanasia, withdrawal of treatment, and physician-assisted death.
  • Evaluate Rachels’ argument that active and passive euthanasia are morally equivalent.
  • Analyze surrogate decision-making standards (substituted judgment vs. best interest).
  • Discuss disability community critiques of MAID and the controversy over psychiatric MAID.

4.8 L8. Justice & Health Care Allocation

  • Compare utilitarian, Rawlsian, libertarian, and capabilities theories of justice.
  • Evaluate arguments for and against a moral right to health care.
  • Compare US, Canadian, German, and UK health systems on key features (coverage, financing, outcomes).
  • Explain documented racial and socioeconomic health disparities and their mechanisms.
  • Analyze implicit bias in clinical encounters and its effects on care.
  • Discuss ethical frameworks for organ allocation and rationing.

4.9 L9. Public Health Ethics

  • Distinguish clinical ethics from public health ethics (different unit of concern, different trade-offs).
  • Analyze tensions between individual liberty and collective welfare in quarantine and vaccine mandates.
  • Evaluate arguments for mandatory vaccination and conscientious exemptions.
  • Apply crisis standards of care to pandemic triage scenarios.
  • Discuss what COVID-19 revealed about existing inequities and governance failures.

4.10 L10. AI in Medicine

  • Explain at a basic level how machine learning diagnostic and predictive tools are built and deployed.
  • Identify sources of algorithmic bias (training data, proxy variables, deployment drift).
  • Analyze the Optum care-management algorithm case and the Epic Sepsis Model controversy.
  • Evaluate informed consent when clinicians rely on black-box AI recommendations.
  • Discuss AI mental-health chatbots and their ethical risks.
  • Consider liability and professional responsibility when AI is in the clinical loop.
  • Examine data-privacy and consent issues specific to health AI.

4.11 L11. Gender, Identity & Disability

  • Describe current gender-affirming care pathways for adults and for adolescents.
  • Summarize the Cass Review and the contested reception of its evidentiary standards.
  • Analyze parental authority, adolescent assent, and clinical gatekeeping in pediatric gender care.
  • Distinguish “mere difference” and “bad difference” views of disability (Barnes vs. Bognar).
  • Evaluate the disability-rights critique of selective abortion (Asch).
  • Discuss body integrity identity disorder and the limits of patient autonomy.
  • Apply the four principles to cases at the intersection of identity and medicine.

4.12 L12. Animal Ethics

  • Compare major views on animal moral status (Kantian, Singer’s utilitarian, Regan’s rights view).
  • Evaluate the 3 Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement) as an ethical framework for animal research.
  • Discuss primate research and the use of animal models for neurological and psychiatric disorders.
  • Analyze the public-health and welfare ethics of industrial animal agriculture.
  • Evaluate xenotransplantation in light of recent pig-heart and pig-kidney clinical trials.
  • Apply moral-status arguments developed in earlier units (abortion, genetics, end-of-life) to animal cases.

5 Case Studies

A companion set of 12 case study documents accompanies the lectures, ordered to follow the course sequence.

# Case Paired Lecture
1 Dax Cowart — refusing burn treatment L2 Clinical relationship
2 Tuskegee Syphilis Study L3 Research ethics
3 Henrietta Lacks & HeLa cells L3 Research ethics (bridges to L8)
4 Kate Cox — post-Dobbs medical exception L4 Abortion
5 Baby M — surrogacy L5 Reproductive tech
6 He Jiankui CRISPR twins (2018) L6 Genetics
7 Terri Schiavo L7 End of life
8 Organ allocation: Sarah Murnaghan & UNOS L8 Justice
9 COVID-19 ventilator triage (NY / Italy) L9 Public health
10 Optum care-management algorithm L10 AI in medicine
11 Bell v. Tavistock L11 Gender, identity & disability
12 David Bennett — first pig-heart xenotransplant L12 Animal ethics
